file_name: Sangers - 2023 - Artificial intelligence in skin cancer smartphone applications.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes a multilevel evaluation of the SkinVision smartphone application, which employs an AI algorithm (specifically a convolutional neural network) to analyze skin lesions for skin cancer detection. The study reports results obtained using this AI-assisted system, including sensitivity (86.9%), specificity (70.4%), and odds ratios for skin cancer claims.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article presents a 'multilevel evaluation' of the SkinVision smartphone application, describing original data collection and analysis across three levels: a clinical study (n=372 patients), a population-based retrospective analysis of health claims (n=18,960 users vs. 56,880 controls), and qualitative focus groups. It reports new empirical results, including sensitivity (86.9%), specificity (70.4%), and odds ratios (1.3 and 3.7), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Artificial intelligence in skin cancer smartphone applications
brief_summary: This study presents a multilevel evaluation of the SkinVision smartphone application, which uses a convolutional neural network to assess skin lesions for skin cancer risk. The first level involved a clinical study of 372 patients with suspicious lesions, demonstrating a sensitivity of 86.9% and a specificity of 70.4%. The second level utilized a population-based retrospective analysis of health claims, comparing 18,960 app users with 56,880 controls to assess real-world impact. Results showed that app users had a moderately higher incidence of (pre)malignant skin lesion claims (OR 1.3) but over three times the risk of benign tumor claims (OR 3.7). The third level included qualitative focus groups revealing public perceptions, barriers, and facilitators regarding AI-assisted skin cancer apps. The authors conclude that while the app improves early detection, its lower specificity raises concerns about overdiagnosis and healthcare resource utilization, necessitating targeted approaches for high-risk populations.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study evaluated the app's ability to detect skin cancer and (pre)malignant lesions. In the clinical study (n=372 patients), the app achieved a sensitivity of 86.9% for detecting skin cancer. In the population-based health claims analysis, app users had a moderately higher incidence of claims for (pre)malignant skin lesions (OR 1.3).

The study analyzed claims for benign conditions to assess specificity and false-positive rates. App users had over three times the risk of filing claims for benign skin tumors and nevi (OR 3.7) compared to controls, indicating a high rate of benign findings flagged by the app.
The clinical study focused on patients with suspicious skin lesions (785 lesions from 372 patients) to assess the app's diagnostic accuracy as an early detection tool.

all_task_types.target: Risk indication (low-risk vs. high-risk) for skin cancer based on lesion photography
all_task_types.details: The SkinVision app employs a convolutional neural network to analyze skin lesions and assigns a 'low- or high-risk indication' to each lesion. This binary risk classification determines whether the user should seek consultation. The study evaluates this classification task by reporting sensitivity (86.9%) and specificity (70.4%) for detecting skin cancer and (pre)malignancies versus benign lesions.

datasets_used.name: Clinical Study Cohort (Erasmus MC and Albert Schweitzer Hospital)
datasets_used.description: A prospective clinical study conducted in secondary care settings at the dermatology outpatient clinics of Erasmus MC Cancer Institute and Albert Schweitzer Hospital in the Netherlands. It involved patients with suspicious skin lesions who used the SkinVision app.

use.testing: Used to assess the app's diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity and specificity) for detecting skin cancer in a clinical setting.
use.other: Validation of the app's performance in a secondary care setting.

datasets_used.name: Population-Based Health Claims Dataset
datasets_used.description: A retrospective analysis of health claims data from a major Dutch health insurer. It compared users of the SkinVision app with a control group to evaluate real-world impact on healthcare utilization.
classes_used: Claims for benign skin tumors and nevi

use.testing: Used to compare incidence of skin cancer and benign lesion claims between app users and controls to assess the app's effect on early detection and overdiagnosis.
use.other: Population-based assessment of app usage and healthcare resource utilization.

datasets_used.name: Qualitative Focus Group Data
datasets_used.description: Data gathered from focus groups with members of the general population in the Netherlands, including both skin cancer app users and non-users, to explore perspectives on AI-assisted skin cancer apps.

use.other: Qualitative research to understand public perception, barriers, and facilitators regarding AI-assisted skin cancer apps.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the incidence of claims for (pre)malignant skin lesions and benign skin tumors between app users and controls in the population-based retrospective analysis.
To assess the diagnostic accuracy of the AI algorithm in detecting skin cancer and (pre)malignancies versus benign lesions in the clinical study.

statistical_methods_used.method: Cost-effectiveness analysis (Cost per additional detected lesion)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the economic impact and efficiency of the app's implementation in terms of detecting (pre)malignancies.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports odds ratios (OR 1.3 and OR 3.7) and confidence intervals are not explicitly provided in the text, but no formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, chi-square tests) or alpha thresholds are reported.

Clinical Study Cohort (Erasmus MC and Al.metric: Sensitivity and Specificity for detecting skin cancer
Clinical Study Cohort (Erasmus MC and Al.notes: The study encompassed 785 lesions from 372 patients with suspicious skin lesions. The lower specificity raised concerns about false positives.

Population-Based Health Claims Dataset.notes: The dataset included 18,960 app users compared to 56,880 controls. App users had a moderately higher incidence of (pre)malignant claims but over three times the risk of benign tumor claims. The cost per additional detected (pre)malignancy was €2567.

Qualitative Focus Group Data.notes: Key perceived barriers to use included lack of value, untrustworthiness, preference for a doctor, privacy concerns, complex interface, and high costs. Key facilitators included high perceived value, transparent/trustworthy developer identity, endorsement by healthcare providers/government, and ease/low costs of use.

classification_or_output_head.details: The algorithm assigns a low- or high-risk indication to each lesion photographed by the user to advise whether the user should seek consultation with a general practitioner or a dermatologist.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SkinVision app (AI algorithm based on convolutional neural network)
conclusions: The SkinVision smartphone app, utilizing a convolutional neural network, demonstrated a sensitivity of 86.9% and a specificity of 70.4% for detecting skin cancer in a clinical study of 372 patients with suspicious lesions.

In a population-based retrospective analysis, app users had a moderately higher incidence of claims for (pre)malignant skin lesions (OR 1.3) but over three times the risk of claims for benign skin tumors and nevi (OR 3.7) compared to controls.
The app's lower specificity raises concerns about overdiagnosis, unnecessary worry, and inefficient use of healthcare resources due to a high rate of false positives in the general population.
The cost per additional detected (pre)malignancy was €2567, suggesting that a targeted approach for high-risk populations may be more cost-effective than widespread use.

Qualitative research identified key barriers to app use, including perceived lack of value, untrustworthiness, privacy concerns, and high costs, while facilitators included high perceived value, transparency, and endorsement by healthcare providers.

To ensure AI-assisted skin cancer apps deliver on their promise, thorough multilevel evaluations are necessary to assess diagnostic accuracy, population-based impact, and stakeholder perspectives, alongside improvements in specificity and alignment with healthcare ecosystem demands.
important_remarks: The app demonstrated a sensitivity of 86.9% but a relatively low specificity of 70.4% in the clinical study (n=372 patients), raising concerns about false positives.

Population-based health claims analysis (n=18,960 users vs. 56,880 controls) revealed that app users had over three times the risk of claims for benign skin tumors/nevi (OR 3.7) compared to (pre)malignant lesions (OR 1.3), indicating a high rate of benign findings flagged by the app.
The cost per additional detected (pre)malignancy was €2567, suggesting that a targeted approach for high-risk populations may be more cost-effective than widespread use in the general population.
The study highlights a potential for overdiagnosis and inefficient use of healthcare resources due to the app's lower specificity when used broadly.

Qualitative focus groups identified key barriers to adoption including perceived lack of value, untrustworthiness, privacy concerns, and high costs, while facilitators included endorsement by healthcare providers and government.

The evaluation was conducted in the Netherlands using data from Erasmus MC Cancer Institute, Albert Schweitzer Hospital, and a major Dutch health insurer, which may limit generalizability to other healthcare systems or populations.
The author reports receiving speaker fees from several pharmaceutical companies and an unrestricted research grant from SkinVision bv, indicating a potential conflict of interest.
